title: The Land Before the Hittites
bullets: Central Anatolia's high plateau at 3,000-4,000 feet with marginal rainfall and brutal winters made agriculture difficult but rich mineral deposits of copper, silver, gold, and iron made the region a magnet for trade.

The indigenous Hattians built sophisticated cities and metalwork traditions in central Anatolia from at least the third millennium BCE, speaking a language isolate unrelated to any known language family.

Royal tombs at Alaca Hoyuk dating to 2400-2200 BCE contained stunning gold and bronze sun disc standards, revealing a wealthy Hattian elite culture with religious traditions centered on solar imagery and animal symbolism.

Assyrian merchants established karum trading colonies across Anatolia around 1950 BCE, with the central hub at Kanesh producing over 23,000 cuneiform tablets documenting an international trade network stretching from Afghanistan to the Aegean.
The Assyrian karum system brought cuneiform writing to Anatolia, giving the Hittites the technology to write their Indo-European language and producing the earliest known written Indo-European texts.

Indo-European speakers appeared in Anatolia by at least 1900 BCE, gradually moving from one population among many to the ruling class while absorbing Hattian culture so thoroughly that they named their kingdom Hatti.
Czech linguist Bedrich Hrozny deciphered Hittite in 1915 by noticing that words for bread, eating, and water resembled Latin and English cognates, revealing the oldest written Indo-European language.
The Hittites were linguistically Indo-European but culturally Anatolian: they wrote in Mesopotamian cuneiform, worshipped Hattian gods, and governed through Near Eastern diplomatic conventions.
subtitle: pre-Hittite Hatti, Assyrian traders, and Anatolia before the empire

title: The First Kings
bullets: King Anitta of Kussara conquered and cursed Hattusa around 1750 BCE, planting cress on the ashes and invoking the Storm God against anyone who rebuilt it, in the earliest known text in the Hittite language.
Hattusili I defied Anitta's curse and refounded Hattusa as his capital around 1650 BCE, choosing the defensible ridge site for its strategic position in the Hattian cultural heartland.
Hattusa grew into one of the largest Bronze Age cities, covering 400 acres with 4-mile double walls, the famous Lions Gate and Sphinx Gate, and a population of 40,000-50,000 at its peak.
Mursili I marched 600 miles from Hattusa to sack Babylon in 1595 BCE, ending Hammurabi's First Babylonian Dynasty in one of the most audacious military raids in ancient history.

The Hittite Old Kingdom descended into a spiral of palace assassinations after Mursili I was murdered by his brother-in-law, with four successive kings dying violently due to the lack of clear succession rules.

The panku noble assembly gave the Hittite aristocracy a voice in governance, creating a political system more dynamic but more unstable than Egypt's divine kingship or Babylon's bureaucratic monarchy.

King Telipinu issued his Edict around 1525 BCE establishing succession rules and forbidding assassinations, one of the earliest surviving attempts to solve a political problem through written constitutional law.
The Middle Hittite Period saw external pressure from Kaska raiders in the north and the Hurrian Mitanni kingdom in the southeast, reducing Hittite power until Suppiluliuma I's dramatic revival.
subtitle: Hattusili I, Mursili I, and the chaotic founding of a great power

title: The Empire Rises
bullets: Suppiluliuma I transformed the Hittites from a second-tier power into a superpower by conquering Mitanni, dominating Syria, and establishing diplomatic equality with Egypt within a single generation.
The conquest of Mitanni around 1340 BCE destroyed the Hurrian superpower, with Suppiluliuma installing his own sons as vassal kings at Aleppo and Carchemish to control the Euphrates corridor.
The Amarna Letters reveal the Hittite king corresponding with the pharaoh as an equal, demanding proper diplomatic respect and protesting when the customary gift exchanges fell short.
An Egyptian queen, probably Tutankhamun's widow Ankhesenamun, wrote begging Suppiluliuma to send a son to become pharaoh, an unprecedented request in three thousand years of Egyptian history.
Prince Zannanza was murdered en route to Egypt, triggering retaliatory Hittite raids into Egyptian territory and bringing back prisoners of war who carried a devastating plague.
The Great Plague of Hatti ravaged the empire for roughly twenty years from 1322 to 1295 BCE, killing Suppiluliuma I himself and drastically weakening the kingdom at the height of its power.

Mursili II's Plague Prayers are among the most emotionally raw religious texts from the ancient world, questioning divine justice and confessing his father's sins while bargaining desperately for relief.
Mursili II held the empire together through relentless campaigning despite the plague, reconquering the Arzawa lands in the west and forcing rebellious vassals back into the Hittite system.
subtitle: Suppiluliuma I transforms a regional kingdom into a superpower

title: The Thousand Gods
bullets: The Hittite pantheon absorbed deities from every conquered and allied people, swelling to over a thousand named gods invoked collectively as the Thousand Gods of Hatti in treaties and oaths.
The Storm God of Hatti was the supreme male deity and patron of kingship, while the Sun Goddess of Arinna was his equal or superior, reflecting the pre-Hittite Hattian solar religious tradition.

The Tawananna office gave the Great Queen independent religious and political authority retained even after her husband's death, making it the most powerful royal female institution in the Bronze Age.
The Hurro-Hittite Song of Kumarbi tells of divine succession through violence, with sky god Anu overthrown by Kumarbi who is overthrown by the Storm God, directly paralleling Hesiod's Greek Theogony.
The open-air rock sanctuary at Yazilikaya features carved reliefs of the entire Hittite pantheon in procession, with a dramatic scene of the god Sharruma embracing King Tudhaliya IV.
The AN.TAH.SUM spring festival lasted 38 days, requiring the king to personally travel across the heartland performing rituals at cult centers to ensure divine favor for the harvest.
Hittite religion was pragmatic rather than dogmatic, focused on correct ritual practice rather than correct belief, with adding foreign gods to the roster understood as divine risk management.

The Hattusa archive contains ritual texts in at least eight languages for curing illness, countering witchcraft, purifying houses, and appeasing angry gods, functioning as detailed instruction manuals for priests.
subtitle: a thousand gods, bilingual rituals, and Hittite religious tolerance

title: Kadesh and the World's First Peace Treaty
bullets: The Battle of Kadesh in 1274 BCE was the largest chariot battle in ancient history, pitting Muwatalli II's heavier three-man Hittite chariots against Ramesses II's lighter two-man Egyptian chariots.

Hittite intelligence agents disguised as Bedouin lured Ramesses into a trap at Kadesh, but the ambush failed when the Hittite chariots paused to loot the Egyptian camp and were hit by a flanking counterattack.
Muwatalli II's controversial move of the capital from Hattusa south to Tarhuntassa created a rival power center that would haunt Hittite politics for generations.

Hattusili III usurped the throne from his nephew around 1267 BCE and wrote the Apology of Hattusili III, the oldest known political autobiography, justifying his coup as sanctioned by the goddess Ishtar.

The Egyptian-Hittite Peace Treaty of 1259 BCE is the oldest known international peace treaty between equals, with mutual defense obligations, refugee provisions, and divine witnesses from both nations.
A copy of the treaty hangs in the United Nations headquarters in New York, recognized as the earliest document embodying the principles of bilateral diplomacy and international law.
Queen Puduhepa co-signed the treaty, conducted independent diplomatic correspondence with both Ramesses II and his queen Nefertari, and negotiated the royal marriage that sealed the peace.
The peace held for the remaining decades of both empires, sealed by a royal marriage in 1246 BCE when Hattusili III's daughter traveled from Hattusa to Egypt to marry Ramesses II.
subtitle: the greatest chariot battle in history and the peace that followed

title: How the Bronze Age Worked
bullets: The Uluburun shipwreck, sunk around 1300 BCE off Turkey's coast, carried cargo from at least seven cultures including 10 tons of Cypriot copper, a ton of Afghan tin, Baltic amber, and a gold scarab of Queen Nefertiti.

The Late Bronze Age international system was held together by three things: long-distance trade in metals and luxury goods, cuneiform as a shared diplomatic language, and chariot armies as the universal military technology.
The Hittites controlled tin routes through Anatolia and Syrian vassal ports like Ugarit, dominating trade flows between Mesopotamia and the Mediterranean in the most strategic commodities.

Kikkuli, a Mitanni horse trainer hired by the Hittite court, wrote the oldest known horse training manual around 1345 BCE, describing a seven-month interval training program that modern equestrians consider sophisticated.
The Hittite law code emphasized restitution over punishment, distinguishing between intentional and accidental harm, in stark contrast to Hammurabi's retributive eye-for-an-eye approach.
Tudhaliya IV revised the law code around 1230 BCE to reduce penalties even further, with the text explicitly noting the changes, demonstrating self-conscious legal reform toward greater leniency.

The Hittites developed iron smelting techniques that gave them a technological edge, with a famous Iron Letter showing the king controlling iron production as a state monopoly sought by foreign rulers.
After the Hittite Empire collapsed, iron technology spread rapidly across the Near East, ending the Bronze Age within a few centuries, possibly driven by the dispersal of Hittite smiths.
subtitle: tin routes, copper ingots, and the international Bronze Age economy

title: The Fall
bullets: The Bronze Age Collapse destroyed or crippled every major eastern Mediterranean power within roughly fifty years, with the Hittite Empire suffering the most complete disappearance of any major civilization.

The Sea Peoples, a diverse collection of displaced populations and raiders from multiple origins, attacked coastal cities across the Mediterranean but were probably a symptom of collapse rather than its sole cause.
Modern scholarship treats the collapse as a systems failure: drought and famine, trade disruption, internal rebellion, earthquakes, and military pressure reinforcing each other in a death spiral.
Desperate letters from the Hittite king begging Egypt for emergency grain shipments reveal severe famine in the empire's final years, as the marginal Anatolian plateau failed under prolonged drought.

The rivalry between Hattusa and the alternate capital at Tarhuntassa may have split the empire internally just as external threats were mounting, with Kurunta possibly briefly seizing the throne itself.
Suppiluliuma II, the last known Hittite king, won a naval victory off Cyprus around 1210 BCE but could not prevent the broader collapse of the trade networks and vassal system sustaining the empire.
Hattusa was deliberately burned around 1178 BCE, probably by Kaska raiders or internal rebels, and was never rebuilt, ending five centuries of Hittite power.
The Hittite vassal system that worked beautifully in good times failed catastrophically in bad ones, lacking the deep institutional bureaucracy that allowed Egypt, Babylon, and Assyria to survive.
subtitle: Sea Peoples, grain shortages, and the Bronze Age Collapse

title: After the Empire
bullets: Neo-Hittite successor states in southeastern Anatolia and northern Syria preserved Hittite culture, Luwian hieroglyphs, and political traditions for five centuries after the empire's collapse.
Carchemish became the leading Neo-Hittite state, its royal dynasty descended from Suppiluliuma I's son, using Hittite titles and maintaining the vassal relationship system of the old empire.

Luwian hieroglyphic script survived the collapse while cuneiform was lost, becoming the primary writing system of the Neo-Hittite world because it could be carved directly onto stone without institutional scribal training.
The Neo-Hittite states blended Hittite, Luwian, and Aramaean cultural elements, with bilingual inscriptions from Sam'al showing how Luwian and Aramaic coexisted in post-imperial communities.
The Neo-Assyrian Empire systematically conquered every Neo-Hittite state by 700 BCE, with Carchemish falling to Sargon II in 717 BCE, ending five centuries of Hittite cultural continuity.

Neo-Hittite monumental sculpture influenced Assyrian art, creating a chain of artistic transmission from the Hattian sun discs through Hittite lions to Neo-Hittite portal sculptures to Assyrian winged bulls.
Hugo Winckler's 1906 excavation of Bogazkoy found thousands of cuneiform tablets including the Egyptian-Hittite Peace Treaty, proving that the ruins were Hattusa, capital of the forgotten empire.

The Hittite archive of over 30,000 readable cuneiform tablets makes them the best-documented lost civilization in history, preserved because the fire that destroyed the city baked the clay tablets into permanent ceramic.
The Hittites pioneered diplomacy-based empire building, inclusive religious syncretism, and restitution-based law, models the later Persian Empire would adopt and vastly expand.
subtitle: Neo-Hittite states survive, then vanish — and modern rediscovery
